did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a shallow convolutional neural network (CNN) named AK-DL to diagnose actinic keratosis (AK) and distinguish it from benign keratosis (BK) using images from the HAM10000 dataset. The model was trained and tested to achieve an accuracy of 0.925, and an intelligent diagnostic iOS app was developed based on this AI model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors designed a new shallow convolutional neural network (AK-DL), trained and tested it on the HAM10000 dataset, compared its performance against other models (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet) and traditional algorithms, and developed an iOS diagnostic app. It includes specific sections for Materials and Methods, Results, and Conclusions, reporting new metrics such as accuracy (0.925) and AUC (0.887).

title: AK-DL: A Shallow Neural Network Model for Diagnosing Actinic Keratosis with Better Performance Than Deep Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study developed a shallow convolutional neural network named AK-DL to diagnose actinic keratosis (AK) and distinguish it from benign keratosis (BK) using images from the HAM10000 dataset. The model was trained and tested on 327 AK and 1099 BK images after preprocessing steps including data augmentation and color space conversion. The authors compared AK-DL's performance against mainstream deep CNNs (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet) and traditional machine learning algorithms. The AK-DL model achieved the highest accuracy of 0.925 and an AUC of 0.887, outperforming the deep CNNs and traditional methods while requiring significantly less training time. Additionally, an intelligent diagnostic iOS app was developed based on the AK-DL model to facilitate real-time and portable diagnosis. The results indicate that shallow CNNs are more effective and efficient than deep CNNs for recognizing actinic keratosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for diagnosis and classification. 327 images from the HAM10000 dataset were used in the experiment.
Used as the negative class for binary classification against AK. 1099 images from the HAM10000 dataset were used. The paper notes that BK includes seborrheic keratosis (SK).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Benign Keratosis (BK)
all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (AK vs BK) using images from the HAM10000 dataset. The task is binary classification, evidenced by the reporting of accuracy (0.925), sensitivity, specificity, precision, MCC, and AUC for distinguishing between the two classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC dataset, collected over 20 years by the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna, Austria, and the skin cancer practice of Cliff Rosendahl in Queensland, Australia. It consists of 100,015 skin images belonging to 7 common skin diseases, with images strictly confirmed by dermatologists. The study used a subset of this dataset containing images of actinic keratosis (AK) and benign keratosis (BK).

use.training: Used for training the AK-DL shallow CNN model after data augmentation (rotation, flipping) and preprocessing (histogram equalization, color space conversion to Lab).
use.testing: Used for testing the AK-DL model and comparing its performance against deep CNNs (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet) and traditional algorithms (SVM, RF, KNN).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's performance by randomly choosing 10% of the data as test data and the rest as training data for 10 times, with mean values of evaluation indexes calculated as final values.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between AK-DL and other models were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, AUC, training time) derived from 10-fold cross-validation without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment_2: Comparison of traditional machine learning algorithms
HAM10000.notes: AK-DL achieved the highest accuracy and AUC among all CNN models. Among traditional algorithms, SVM had the best performance (Accuracy 0.791, AUC 0.749). AK-DL training time was significantly lower than deep CNNs but higher than traditional algorithms.

architecture_used.main_model: AK-DL (Actinic Keratosis Deep Learning), a shallow convolutional neural network (CNN)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported for AK-DL; AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet used transfer learning with ImageNet pretraining

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (implied Cross-Entropy for classification)
training_details.hardware: Windows 10, CPU Intel Core I7-6700HQ (3.5 GHz, 4 GB memory)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully Connected Layer followed by Softmax

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: rotation (90 degrees), horizontal flipping, vertical flipping
Color space conversion: RGB to Lab color space

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The combination of rotation, horizontal flipping, and vertical flipping was applied to the limited AK images to produce 1308 derived samples, addressing class imbalance.

conclusions: The proposed shallow convolutional neural network (AK-DL) achieved superior performance compared to mainstream deep CNNs (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet) and traditional machine learning algorithms (SVM, RF, KNN) for diagnosing actinic keratosis (AK) versus benign keratosis (BK).
AK-DL demonstrated the highest accuracy (0.925) and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.887 among all tested models.

AK-DL required significantly less training time (123.0 seconds) compared to deep CNNs (AlexNet: 2426.0 s, GoogLeNet: 13761.0 s, ResNet: 15488.0 s), indicating higher practical value for commercialization and rapid diagnosis.
An intelligent diagnostic iOS application was successfully developed based on the AK-DL model to facilitate real-time and portable diagnosis of AK.
The study concludes that employing a shallow CNN is more effective and efficient than deep CNNs for the recognition of actinic keratosis.

A limitation of the study is that the model was only validated on images of AK and BK; it cannot detect precancerous skin in areas not yet visibly affected by AK due to a lack of relevant training images for other precancerous lesions.
Future work requires a large-scale database covering different precancerous skin lesions to further train and evaluate algorithms for real clinical settings.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Actinic Keratosis vs. Benign Keratosis) and does not address other precancerous skin lesions, limiting its generalizability to broader clinical screening.
The model was trained and validated exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset; lack of external validation on independent datasets raises concerns about potential dataset bias and generalizability.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for performance comparisons between AK-DL and other models, relying solely on descriptive statistics.

The dataset used for training was small (1,426 images total) and imbalanced (327 AK vs. 1,099 BK), necessitating data augmentation which may introduce bias or overfitting risks despite the use of dropout.
The iOS diagnostic app was developed using a server-side architecture (PHP/Python) rather than pure on-device inference, which impacts real-time performance and privacy considerations.

The study claims superior performance of shallow CNNs over deep CNNs, but this conclusion is specific to the texture features of keratosis images and may not apply to other dermatological conditions requiring complex feature extraction.
Hardware specifications for the iOS app deployment are not detailed, making it difficult to assess the model's efficiency on actual mobile devices versus the reported server-side training times.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized a subset of the HAM10000 dataset (from the ISIC archive) containing 1,426 dermoscopic images of Actinic Keratosis (AK, n=327) and Benign Keratosis (BK, n=1,099). The dataset was used for both training and testing via a 10-fold cross-validation protocol. Data augmentation (rotation, flipping) was applied to the limited AK class to generate 1,308 additional samples. No external or independent test set was used; all performance metrics were derived from the cross-validation folds on this single source.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset subset. The authors explicitly state in the limitations that the model was only validated on images of AK and BK from this dataset and that a large-scale database covering different precancerous skin lesions is needed for future clinical settings.

training.counts: 1,426 images total (327 AK, 1,099 BK) before augmentation; augmented to include 1,308 derived AK samples
training.notes: Used for model fitting via 10-fold cross-validation. Data augmentation was applied to the AK class to address class imbalance.

validation.counts: 10% of data per fold (part of 10-fold CV)
validation.notes: Used for model selection/tuning within the cross-validation loop. The paper does not report a separate held-out validation set distinct from the CV procedure.

testing.counts: 10% of data per fold (part of 10-fold CV)
testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation. Results are reported as mean values across the 10 folds.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The AK-DL model was built from scratch. However, AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet were used as baseline comparisons, which typically utilize ImageNet pretraining, though the paper does not explicitly confirm the pretraining source for these baselines in the context of this specific experiment.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation was performed on the 327 original AK images using rotation (90 degrees), horizontal flipping, and vertical flipping to produce 1,308 derived samples, addressing class imbalance.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 10-fold cross-validation. In each fold, 10% of the data was randomly chosen as test data and the remaining 90% as training data. Mean values of evaluation indexes from the 10 folds were calculated as final values.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: 10-fold cross-validation performed on the single HAM10000 subset. Folds stayed within one dataset.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The study acknowledges this limitation, stating that the model was only confirmed in the keratosis dataset and lacks validation on other precancerous lesions.

dataset_level_results.notes: AK-DL achieved the highest accuracy and AUC among all tested CNN models. Results are mean values from 10-fold cross-validation.
Best performing traditional machine learning algorithm.

generalizability_notes: The study is limited to a binary classification task (AK vs BK) using only the HAM10000 dataset.
Lack of external validation on independent datasets raises concerns about potential dataset bias and generalizability.
The model cannot detect precancerous skin in areas not yet visibly affected by AK due to a lack of relevant training images for other precancerous lesions.
The dataset was small (1,426 images) and imbalanced (327 AK vs 1,099 BK), necessitating data augmentation which may introduce bias or overfitting risks.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for performance comparisons.
information_not_reported: Specific patient-level separation details (whether images from the same patient were split across folds).
Exact number of images in each of the 10 training/test folds (only percentages are given).
Pretraining source details for the deep CNN baselines (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet) used in this specific study.
Confidence intervals for the reported metrics.
